fact_summary: An uncle promised his nephew $5,000 if the nephew refrained from drinking, smoking, and gambling until age 21. The nephew performed, but the uncle died before paying. The executor refused payment, claiming no consideration.
legal_question: Does forbearance from legal rights constitute valid consideration for a unilateral contract?
us_claim: The nephew's assignee can enforce the promise because forbearance of legal rights is valid consideration.
us_legal_basis: Under the benefit-detriment theory, consideration may consist of a benefit to the promisor or a detriment to the promisee. Forbearance from lawful conduct at the promisor's request constitutes legal detriment sufficient to support a contract.
us_sources: Hamer v. Sidway, 124 N.Y. 538, 27 N.E. 256 (N.Y. 1891)
Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 71 (1981)
Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 79 (1981)

fact_summary: Two men discussed selling a farm over drinks. The seller wrote and signed a memorandum on a restaurant check agreeing to sell for $50,000. The buyer arranged financing and hired counsel, but the seller later claimed it was a joke.
legal_question: Is a contract enforceable when one party claims subjective lack of intent, but the other party reasonably believed there was assent?
us_claim: The contract is enforceable because contract formation turns on objective manifestations of assent, not undisclosed subjective intent.
us_legal_basis: The objective theory of contract holds that a party's secret reservations or unexpressed intentions are immaterial if his words and acts would lead a reasonable person to believe a real agreement was intended. Specific performance is the standard remedy for breach of land-sale contracts.
us_sources: Lucy v. Zehmer, 196 Va. 493, 84 S.E.2d 516 (Va. 1954)
Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 17 (1981)
Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 20 (1981)

fact_summary: A buyer sent a purchase order for widgets. The seller sent an acknowledgment with additional terms, including a warranty disclaimer. Both parties are merchants and proceeded with shipment and payment.
legal_question: Do additional terms in an acceptance become part of the contract between merchants under the UCC?
us_claim: The warranty disclaimer is a material alteration and does not become part of the contract unless expressly agreed to; the contract is formed by conduct under UCC § 2-207(3).
us_legal_basis: UCC § 2-207 governs the 'battle of the forms.' Between merchants, additional terms become part of the contract unless they materially alter the contract, the offer expressly limits acceptance to its terms, or objection is given. Conflicting terms may 'knock out' under the common law approach adopted in many jurisdictions.
us_sources: U.C.C. § 2-207 (Am. L. Inst. & Unif. L. Comm'n 2024)
Dorton v. Collins & Aikman Corp., 453 F.2d 1161 (6th Cir. 1972)
Klocek v. Gateway, Inc., 104 F. Supp. 2d 1332 (D. Kan. 2000)

fact_summary: A passenger carrying a package of fireworks ran to board a train. Railroad guards helped him board, causing him to drop the package. The explosion caused scales at the other end of the platform to fall on Mrs. Palsgraf, injuring her.
legal_question: Does a railroad owe a duty of care to a bystander injured by an unforeseeable chain of events?
us_claim: The railroad is not liable because it owed no duty to Palsgraf; her injury was not a reasonably foreseeable consequence of the guards' conduct.
us_legal_basis: Negligence is relational; liability requires breach of a duty owed to the particular plaintiff. The scope of duty is defined by foreseeable risk: 'The risk reasonably to be perceived defines the duty to be obeyed.' If a plaintiff is outside the range of apprehension created by the defendant's conduct, the defendant has not breached a duty to that plaintiff.
us_sources: Palsgraf v. Long Island Railroad Co., 248 N.Y. 339, 162 N.E. 99 (N.Y. 1928)
Restatement (Second) of Torts § 281 (1965)
Restatement (Third) of Torts: Liability for Physical and Emotional Harm § 7 (2010)

fact_summary: A manufacturer sold cars with undisclosed minor cosmetic repairs as 'new.' A buyer discovered the repainting and sued. The jury awarded $4,000 in compensatory damages and $4,000,000 in punitive damages.
legal_question: What constitutional limits apply to punitive damages awards under the Due Process Clause?
us_claim: The punitive damages award is unconstitutionally excessive because it bears no reasonable relationship to the harm and exceeds the guideposts for constitutional proportionality.
us_legal_basis: The U.S. Supreme Court established three 'guideposts' for reviewing punitive damages: (1) the degree of reprehensibility of the defendant's misconduct; (2) the disparity between the actual or potential harm and the punitive award; (3) the difference between the punitive damages and civil penalties authorized in comparable cases. Few awards exceeding a single-digit ratio between punitive and compensatory damages will satisfy due process.
us_sources: BMW of North America, Inc. v. Gore, 517 U.S. 559 (1996)
State Farm Mut. Auto. Ins. Co. v. Campbell, 538 U.S. 408 (2003)
Philip Morris USA, Inc. v. Williams, 549 U.S. 346 (2007)

fact_summary: An employee was injured on the job and filed a workers' compensation claim. The employer terminated the employee shortly after the claim was filed.
legal_question: Does the public policy exception to at-will employment protect employees terminated for filing workers' compensation claims?
us_claim: The termination violates public policy and constitutes wrongful discharge because it penalizes the employee for exercising a statutory right.
us_legal_basis: The public policy exception to at-will employment bars termination when the discharge would contravene a well-established public policy of the state. Most states recognize that firing an employee for filing a workers' compensation claim or refusing to violate the law violates public policy.
us_sources: Petermann v. International Brotherhood of Teamsters, 344 P.2d 25 (Cal. Ct. App. 1959)
Brockmeyer v. Dun & Bradstreet, 113 Wis. 2d 561 (Wis. 1983)
Restatement (Third) of Employment Law § 4.01 (2015)

fact_summary: A shareholder filed a derivative action challenging a board-approved employment contract for a 47% stockholder/director that included substantial salary, bonuses, and interest-free loans. The shareholder did not make a pre-suit demand.
legal_question: When is a shareholder's pre-suit demand excused as futile in a derivative action challenging a board decision?
us_claim: Demand is excused only if particularized facts create a reasonable doubt that (1) the directors are disinterested and independent, or (2) the challenged transaction was the product of a valid exercise of business judgment.
us_legal_basis: Under Delaware law, the business judgment rule presumes directors acted on an informed basis, in good faith, and in the honest belief their decision served the corporation's interests. A complaint must plead particularized facts rebutting this presumption to bypass the demand requirement under Court of Chancery Rule 23.1.
us_sources: Aronson v. Lewis, 473 A.2d 805 (Del. 1984)
Brehm v. Eisner, 746 A.2d 244 (Del. 2000)
Del. Ch. Ct. R. 23.1

fact_summary: A controlling stockholder negotiated a merger with the corporation and obtained a majority of the board's approval. Minority shareholders challenged the transaction as unfair.
legal_question: What standard of review applies when a controlling stockholder engages in a conflicted transaction with the corporation?
us_claim: The transaction is subject to entire fairness review because the controlling stockholder stood on both sides of the transaction and extracted a non-pro rata benefit.
us_legal_basis: When a controlling stockholder engages in a self-dealing transaction, the business judgment rule does not apply. The transaction is reviewed for entire fairness, requiring the defendant to show both fair dealing and fair price. Alternatively, the controller can obtain approval by fully informed, uncoerced minority shareholders or independent directors to shift the standard of review.
us_sources: Weinberger v. UOP, Inc., 457 A.2d 701 (Del. 1983)
Kahn v. M&F Worldwide Corp., 88 A.3d 635 (Del. 2014)
Corwin v. KKR Financial Holdings LLC, 125 A.3d 304 (Del. 2015)

fact_summary: A corporation's board rejected a hostile takeover bid, citing concerns about undervaluation and long-term strategic value. The bidder sued, claiming the board was entrenching itself.
legal_question: What standard governs a board's decision to resist a hostile takeover under Delaware law?
us_claim: The board's defensive measures are valid if they are reasonable in relation to the threat posed and are not preclusive or coercive.
us_legal_basis: Under the Unocal standard, a board may implement defensive measures if it has reasonable grounds for believing a danger to corporate policy and effectiveness existed, and the defensive measures are proportional to the threat. Under Revlon, when a sale of the company is inevitable, the board must maximize shareholder value.
us_sources: Unocal Corp. v. Mesa Petroleum Co., 493 A.2d 946 (Del. 1985)
Revlon, Inc. v. MacAndrews & Forbes Holdings, Inc., 506 A.2d 173 (Del. 1986)
Unitrin, Inc. v. American General Corp., 651 A.2d 1361 (Del. 1995)

fact_summary: Police arrested a suspect and interrogated him for two hours without informing him of his right to remain silent or to counsel. The suspect confessed orally and signed a written statement.
legal_question: Are statements obtained during custodial interrogation without Miranda warnings admissible at trial?
us_claim: The confession is inadmissible because the Fifth Amendment privilege against self-incrimination requires procedural safeguards during custodial interrogation.
us_legal_basis: Miranda warnings are required before custodial interrogation: the right to remain silent, that anything said can be used in court, the right to an attorney, and the right to appointed counsel if indigent. The government bears a heavy burden to demonstrate that any waiver was knowing, intelligent, and voluntary.
us_sources: Miranda v. Arizona, 384 U.S. 436 (1966)
Dickerson v. United States, 530 U.S. 428 (2000)
Berghuis v. Thompkins, 560 U.S. 370 (2010)

fact_summary: A prosecutor offered a defendant a plea to a lesser charge. When the defendant refused, the prosecutor obtained a new indictment adding a habitual offender charge that carried a life sentence. The defendant pleaded guilty under the new indictment.
legal_question: Does a prosecutor violate due process by threatening to bring more severe charges if the defendant refuses to plead guilty?
us_claim: The prosecutor did not violate due process because the defendant was plainly subject to prosecution on the more serious charge, and plea bargaining is a constitutionally legitimate 'give-and-take' process.
us_legal_basis: A prosecutor has broad discretion in charging decisions. Threatening to bring additional charges during plea negotiations does not violate due process so long as the defendant is free to accept or reject the offer and the charges are supported by probable cause. The plea bargaining process is essential to the functioning of the criminal justice system.
us_sources: Bordenkircher v. Hayes, 434 U.S. 357 (1978)
Brady v. United States, 397 U.S. 742 (1970)
North Carolina v. Pearce, 395 U.S. 711 (1969)

fact_summary: A defendant was convicted of murder based largely on a co-conspirator's testimony. The prosecution failed to disclose that the co-conspirator had been promised immunity in exchange for his testimony.
legal_question: Does the prosecution's failure to disclose an immunity deal with a key witness violate due process?
us_claim: The conviction must be reversed because the prosecution's failure to disclose the immunity agreement violated Brady and Giglio.
us_legal_basis: Under Brady v. Maryland, the prosecution must disclose material exculpatory evidence to the defense. Under Giglio v. United States, this duty extends to impeachment evidence that could affect witness credibility. An immunity deal is material because it may affect the witness' credibility in the eyes of the jury.
us_sources: Brady v. Maryland, 373 U.S. 83 (1963)
Giglio v. United States, 405 U.S. 150 (1972)
Kyles v. Whitley, 514 U.S. 419 (1995)

fact_summary: The EPA interpreted the term 'source' of air pollution under the Clean Air Act to allow a plantwide definition (the 'bubble concept'), meaning modifications within a plant did not require permits if overall emissions did not increase. Environmental groups challenged the interpretation.
legal_question: How much deference should courts give to an agency's interpretation of an ambiguous statute it administers?
us_claim: The EPA's interpretation is reasonable and entitled to deference because Congress left a gap for the agency to fill.
us_legal_basis: Under the Chevron doctrine, if a statute is silent or ambiguous on a specific issue, courts must defer to the agency's reasonable interpretation. The agency's interpretation represents a reasonable accommodation of competing interests and is entitled to controlling weight unless arbitrary, capricious, or manifestly contrary to the statute.
us_sources: Chevron U.S.A., Inc. v. Natural Resources Defense Council, Inc., 467 U.S. 837 (1984)
Mead Corp. v. United States, 533 U.S. 218 (2001)
Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo, 603 U.S. ___ (2024) (overruling Chevron)

fact_summary: An agency issued a rule defining the scope of a statutory exemption after notice-and-comment rulemaking. A regulated entity challenged the rule as exceeding the agency's statutory authority.
legal_question: What standard of review applies to an agency's legislative rule interpreting its governing statute?
us_claim: The rule is valid if it is a reasonable interpretation of the statute and was promulgated through proper notice-and-comment procedures.
us_legal_basis: Legislative rules have the force of law and are reviewed under the arbitrary and capricious standard of the Administrative Procedure Act (APA), 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(A). Courts give deference to the agency's expertise and policy choices when the statute is ambiguous. However, under the Major Questions Doctrine, agency actions of vast economic and political significance require a much clearer authorization from Congress.
us_sources: 5 U.S.C. § 553 (APA Rulemaking)
5 U.S.C. § 706 (APA Judicial Review)
West Virginia v. EPA, 597 U.S. 697 (2022)

fact_summary: A federal agency denied a Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) request for documents related to an ongoing enforcement investigation, citing an exemption for law enforcement records that could reasonably be expected to interfere with enforcement proceedings.
legal_question: Does the FOIA exemption for law enforcement records apply to documents from an ongoing investigation?
us_claim: The agency properly withheld the documents under FOIA Exemption 7(A) because disclosure could reasonably be expected to interfere with the ongoing enforcement proceeding.
us_legal_basis: FOIA Exemption 7(A), 5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(7)(A), protects records or information compiled for law enforcement purposes if disclosure could reasonably be expected to interfere with enforcement proceedings. The exemption is discretionary and evaporates once the investigation is concluded or the proceeding is no longer prospective.
us_sources: NLRB v. Robbins Tire & Rubber Co., 437 U.S. 214 (1978)
U.S. Dep't of Justice v. Reporters Comm. for Freedom of the Press, 489 U.S. 749 (1989)

fact_summary: A for-profit business collected personal information from California residents, including names, addresses, and browsing history, without providing notice of the categories collected or the purposes for use. A consumer requested disclosure and deletion of her personal information, which the business denied.
legal_question: Does a business violate the CCPA by failing to provide notice at collection and refusing a consumer's verifiable request for disclosure and deletion?
us_claim: The business violated the CCPA by failing to inform consumers of the categories of personal information collected and the purposes for use, and by failing to respond to the consumer's verifiable request.
us_legal_basis: The California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) requires businesses to inform consumers at or before the point of collection of the categories of personal information to be collected and the purposes for which they will be used. Consumers have the right to request disclosure of personal information collected and the right to request deletion. Businesses must respond to verifiable consumer requests within 45 days.
us_sources: Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.100 (CCPA Notice at Collection)
Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.105 (CCPA Right to Deletion)
Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.110 (CCPA Right to Know)

fact_summary: A company experienced a data breach exposing unencrypted personal information of 100,000 California residents, including Social Security numbers and financial account numbers. The company failed to implement reasonable security measures.
legal_question: Can consumers sue for statutory damages under the CCPA for a data breach caused by a business's failure to maintain reasonable security?
us_claim: Consumers may bring a private right of action for statutory damages of $100-$750 per incident if the business failed to maintain reasonable security procedures and practices.
us_legal_basis: CCPA § 1798.150 provides a private right of action for consumers whose nonencrypted or nonredacted personal information is subject to unauthorized access due to a business's violation of the duty to implement and maintain reasonable security procedures. Consumers need not prove actual damages to recover statutory damages between $100 and $750 per consumer per incident.
us_sources: Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.150 (CCPA Private Right of Action)
Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.150(a)(1)
Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.81.5 (Duty to Maintain Security)

fact_summary: A social media platform sold users' personal information to third-party advertisers without providing a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link or obtaining opt-out consent. Users were not informed of the sale.
legal_question: Does selling personal information without an opt-out mechanism violate the CCPA?
us_claim: The platform violated the CCPA by selling personal information without providing consumers the right to opt out and by failing to post the required 'Do Not Sell' link.
us_legal_basis: The CCPA grants consumers the right to opt out of the sale of their personal information. Businesses that sell personal information must provide a clear and conspicuous link titled 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' on their homepage. A 'sale' is broadly defined as any transfer of personal information for monetary or other valuable consideration.
us_sources: Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.120 (CCPA Right to Opt-Out)
Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.135 (CCPA 'Do Not Sell' Link)
Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.140(ad) (Definition of 'Sale')

fact_summary: A rap music group created a parody of a famous rock ballad, using the original's opening bass riff and first line of lyrics but replacing the romantic content with bawdy, satirical lyrics. The copyright holder sued for infringement.
legal_question: Does a commercial parody of a copyrighted song qualify as fair use?
us_claim: The parody is fair use because it comments on and criticizes the original work, and the commercial nature of the use does not create a presumption against fair use.
us_legal_basis: Under 17 U.S.C. § 107, fair use is determined by four factors: (1) purpose and character of the use; (2) nature of the copyrighted work; (3) amount and substantiality of the portion used; and (4) effect on the potential market. A parody is transformative because it adds new expression, meaning, or message. The more transformative the new work, the less significant other factors like commercialism become.
us_sources: Campbell v. Acuff-Rose Music, Inc., 510 U.S. 569 (1994)
17 U.S.C. § 107 (Fair Use)
Warhol Foundation v. Goldsmith, 598 U.S. ___ (2023)

fact_summary: A patent claimed an adjustable pedal assembly with an electronic sensor attached to a fixed pivot point. Prior art disclosed adjustable pedals and electronic sensors separately, but not in the exact combination.
legal_question: Is a patent claim obvious when it merely combines known elements in a predictable way?
us_claim: The claim is obvious because mounting an available sensor on a fixed pivot point of a known adjustable pedal was a design step well within the grasp of a person of ordinary skill.
us_legal_basis: Under 35 U.S.C. § 103, a patent claim is invalid if the differences between the subject matter sought to be patented and the prior art are such that the subject matter as a whole would have been obvious at the time the invention was made. The Supreme Court rejected the rigid 'teaching, suggestion, or motivation' (TSM) test and required an 'expansive and flexible approach' that considers common sense, design incentives, and market forces.
us_sources: KSR International Co. v. Teleflex Inc., 550 U.S. 398 (2007)
Graham v. John Deere Co., 383 U.S. 1 (1966)
35 U.S.C. § 103 (Obviousness)

fact_summary: A new company began selling coffee under a mark similar to a famous coffeehouse chain's trademark. The marks were visually and phonetically similar, and both companies sold coffee and related merchandise.
legal_question: What factors determine whether there is a likelihood of confusion between two trademarks?
us_claim: There is a likelihood of confusion because the marks are similar, the products are closely related, and the senior mark is strong and distinctive.
us_legal_basis: The Second Circuit's Polaroid factors assess likelihood of confusion by weighing: (1) strength of the senior mark; (2) similarity of the marks; (3) proximity of the products; (4) likelihood the senior owner will bridge the gap; (5) evidence of actual confusion; (6) defendant's good faith; (7) quality of defendant's product; and (8) sophistication of the buyers. No single factor is dispositive.
us_sources: Polaroid Corp. v. Polarad Electronics Corp., 287 F.2d 492 (2d Cir. 1961)
15 U.S.C. § 1114(1)(a) (Lanham Act Infringement)
Starbucks Corp. v. Wolfe's Borough Coffee, Inc., 736 F.3d 198 (2d Cir. 2013)

fact_summary: An employee with 15 years of service was terminated without cause. The employee handbook stated that employees would be terminated only for just cause and listed a progressive discipline procedure, but contained a disclaimer stating it was not a contract.
legal_question: Can an employee handbook create an implied contract exception to at-will employment?
us_claim: The handbook may create an implied contract if its provisions reasonably give the employee an expectation of job security, despite the disclaimer, depending on state law.
us_legal_basis: The implied contract exception to at-will employment arises when an employer's policies, handbooks, or oral assurances create a reasonable expectation that employment will continue except for just cause. Whether a disclaimer negates this expectation depends on its prominence, clarity, and whether it contradicts the substantive provisions. Thirty-eight states recognize some form of the implied contract exception.
us_sources: Toussaint v. Blue Cross & Blue Shield, 292 N.W.2d 880 (Mich. 1980)
California Labor Code § 2922 (At-Will Presumption)
Restatement (Third) of Employment Law § 4.02 (2015)

fact_summary: An employer required all employees to sign non-compete agreements prohibiting them from working for any competitor within 100 miles for two years after termination, regardless of position or access to trade secrets.
legal_question: Are overbroad non-compete agreements enforceable under state law?
us_claim: The non-compete is likely unenforceable because it is overbroad in geographic scope and duration, and applies to all employees regardless of legitimate business interests.
us_legal_basis: Non-compete agreements are generally disfavored as restraints on trade. They are enforceable only if they protect a legitimate business interest (such as trade secrets or customer relationships), are reasonable in geographic scope and duration, and do not impose undue hardship on the employee. The FTC has also issued a rule banning most non-competes nationwide.
us_sources: FTC Rule 16 CFR Part 910 (Non-Compete Clause Rule, 2024)
BDO USA, LLP v. Green, 879 F.3d 471 (2d Cir. 2018)
Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 188 (1981)

fact_summary: An employee was terminated after reporting safety violations to OSHA. The employer claimed the termination was due to poor performance, but the employee had consistently positive performance reviews.
legal_question: Does the whistleblower protection under OSHA's anti-retaliation provision protect employees who report safety violations?
us_claim: The termination violates the whistleblower protection provision because it was retaliatory and the employee engaged in protected activity by reporting safety violations.
us_legal_basis: Under the Occupational Safety and Health Act (OSHA), 29 U.S.C. § 660(c), employers are prohibited from discharging or discriminating against employees because they filed a complaint or instituted any proceeding related to safety violations. The employee must show: (1) engagement in protected activity; (2) the employer knew of the activity; (3) adverse action; and (4) a causal connection between the protected activity and the adverse action.
us_sources: 29 U.S.C. § 660(c) (OSHA Anti-Retaliation)
29 C.F.R. § 1977.6 (OSHA Whistleblower Regulations)
Burlington Northern & Santa Fe Ry. Co. v. White, 548 U.S. 53 (2006)

fact_summary: A Ku Klux Klan leader gave a speech at a rally warning of 'revengeance' if the government continued to suppress the white race. He was convicted under Ohio's criminal syndicalism statute for advocating violence.
legal_question: Does the First Amendment protect advocacy of force or law violation?
us_claim: The conviction violates the First Amendment because the statute punishes mere advocacy rather than incitement to imminent lawless action.
us_legal_basis: The First Amendment protects advocacy of the use of force or of law violation except where such advocacy is directed to inciting or producing imminent lawless action and is likely to incite or produce such action. Mere abstract teaching of the moral propriety of violence, without preparation for immediate violence, is protected speech.
us_sources: Brandenburg v. Ohio, 395 U.S. 444 (1969)
Whitney v. California, 274 U.S. 357 (1927) (overruled by Brandenburg)
Schenck v. United States, 249 U.S. 47 (1919)

fact_summary: A state law required public school teachers to lead students in a voluntary prayer at the beginning of each school day. A group of parents challenged the law as an establishment of religion.
legal_question: Does a state law authorizing prayer in public schools violate the Establishment Clause?
us_claim: The law violates the Establishment Clause because it constitutes a government endorsement of religion in public schools.
us_legal_basis: The Establishment Clause of the First Amendment, applied to the states through the Fourteenth Amendment, prohibits government from making any law respecting an establishment of religion. Government-sponsored prayer in public schools, even if denominated as voluntary, constitutes an impermissible endorsement of religion because it places students in the position of participating in or observing a religious exercise.
us_sources: Engel v. Vitale, 370 U.S. 421 (1962)
Lee v. Weisman, 505 U.S. 577 (1992)
Town of Greece v. Galloway, 572 U.S. 565 (2014)

fact_summary: Police installed a GPS tracking device on a suspect's vehicle and monitored his movements for 28 days without a warrant. The suspect was later indicted for drug trafficking based on location data.
legal_question: Does warrantless GPS tracking of a vehicle for an extended period violate the Fourth Amendment?
us_claim: The GPS tracking violated the Fourth Amendment because it constituted a search by monitoring the suspect's movements in detail over an extended period.
us_legal_basis: The Fourth Amendment protects against unreasonable searches and seizures. The government's installation of a GPS device on a vehicle and use of that device to monitor the vehicle's movements constitutes a search under the Fourth Amendment. Prolonged monitoring reveals an intimate picture of the subject's life and requires a warrant supported by probable cause.
us_sources: United States v. Jones, 565 U.S. 400 (2012)
Carpenter v. United States, 585 U.S. ___ (2018)
Katz v. United States, 389 U.S. 347 (1967)

fact_summary: A Washington state court sought to exercise personal jurisdiction over a Delaware corporation that sold shoes in Washington but had no office or agents there. The corporation challenged jurisdiction.
legal_question: What constitutional standard governs a state court's exercise of personal jurisdiction over a nonresident corporation?
us_claim: The state may exercise jurisdiction if the defendant has 'minimum contacts' with the forum such that the suit does not offend 'traditional notions of fair play and substantial justice.'
us_legal_basis: Due process requires that a nonresident defendant have certain minimum contacts with the forum state so that maintenance of the suit does not offend traditional notions of fair play and substantial justice. Minimum contacts can be specific (arising from the defendant's forum-related activities) or general (continuous and systematic contacts). The defendant must have purposefully availed itself of the privilege of conducting activities within the forum.
us_sources: International Shoe Co. v. Washington, 326 U.S. 310 (1945)
World-Wide Volkswagen Corp. v. Woodson, 444 U.S. 286 (1980)
Burger King Corp. v. Rudzewicz, 471 U.S. 462 (1985)

fact_summary: In a federal civil case, the plaintiff served broad discovery requests seeking all documents related to the defendant's business operations over the past 10 years. The defendant objected, claiming the requests were overly broad and burdensome.
legal_question: What limits apply to the scope of discovery under the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure?
us_claim: Discovery is limited to nonprivileged matters that are relevant to any party's claim or defense and proportional to the needs of the case.
us_legal_basis: FRCP 26(b)(1) defines the scope of discovery as any nonprivileged matter relevant to any party's claim or defense and proportional to the needs of the case, considering: importance of issues, amount in controversy, parties' relative access to information, parties' resources, importance of discovery in resolving issues, and whether burden outweighs likely benefit. Courts may issue protective orders under FRCP 26(c) to limit discovery.
us_sources: Fed. R. Civ. P. 26(b)(1) (Scope of Discovery)
Fed. R. Civ. P. 26(c) (Protective Orders)
Fed. R. Civ. P. 26(b)(2) (Limitations on Frequency and Extent)

fact_summary: A plaintiff in a class action sought to represent 1.5 million female employees of a national retailer, alleging a pattern and practice of sex discrimination in pay and promotions. The plaintiffs claimed a common policy existed across all stores.
legal_question: What is required for a class action to be certified under Rule 23 when the proposed class is extremely large and geographically dispersed?
us_claim: The class cannot be certified because the plaintiffs failed to show a common question of law or fact across all stores, and individual issues predominated.
us_legal_basis: Under FRCP 23(a), a class must satisfy numerosity, commonality, typicality, and adequacy. Under Rule 23(b)(3), common questions must predominate over individual questions. Mere presence of a common policy is insufficient; plaintiffs must demonstrate that the policy operated uniformly across all class members to generate common questions. Statistical evidence of disparities, without proof of a uniform policy, cannot establish commonality.
us_sources: Wal-Mart Stores, Inc. v. Dukes, 564 U.S. 338 (2011)
Fed. R. Civ. P. 23(a) (Class Certification Prerequisites)
Fed. R. Civ. P. 23(b)(3) (Predominance and Superiority)
